text: The video shows a left hydrosalpinx and a left chocolate cyst, with intestines severely adherent to the left side posterior to the uterus and the left pelvic wall. The uterus is immobile despite the use of a uterine manipulator, indicating a total frozen pelvis.
The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to desiccate the hydrosalpinx on the left pelvic side, followed by cutting with scissors to create space in the area.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to desiccate and separate intestinal adhesions from the left pelvic wall, carefully directing the dissection towards the pelvic wall rather than the intestines.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect and separate the adherent intestine from the surrounding tissue, proceeding gradually and gently to avoid injury.
The surgeon identifies the intestine that is adherent to surrounding structures, emphasizing the need for careful manipulation during the adhesiolysis procedure.

The surgeon uses a Maryland forceps to carefully dissect and separate the intestine from the posterior uterine wall and pelvic wall, ensuring the forceps are positioned on the left side away from the intestinal side.
The surgeon carefully dissects the space between the adherent intestine and the posterior uterine wall using a Maryland forceps, ensuring precise separation of the tissues.
The surgeon remains lateral to the lateral uterine wall, progressing towards the hydrosalpinx.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clean the surgical field, revealing a tubular structure amidst the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully dissects the area, potentially encountering a chocolate cyst, while ensuring that the tissue being cut is properly identified. A suction irrigation cannula is prepared to manage any leakage of chocolate fluid during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the left hydrosalpinx tube, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a portion of the chocolate cyst located at the terminal end of the tubular structure, with visible dark fluid consistent with the cyst's contents.
The surgeon plans to remove the left-sided salpingectomy and addresses the left adnexal mass, with the rectum visible in the midline of the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to make an incision on the retroperitoneal tissue, focusing on the area adjacent to the left hydrosalpinx.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter and internal iliac artery, ensuring these structures are clearly delineated during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies two tubular structures, including a hydrosalpinx, within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a salpingectomy on the rectum using bipolar forceps and scissors, carefully dissecting the tissue.
The surgeon removes an instrument through the main umbilical port using a 5mm laparoscopic scope.
The surgeon performs morselation to remove tissue through the tenement port, using laparoscopic instruments to facilitate the extraction.

The surgeon performs copious suction using a suction device, while a rectal probe is placed to ensure the rectum is not injured. The ureter and pelvic vessels are carefully visualized to confirm their integrity. The video shows the left side with severe intestinal adhesions, contrasting with the previously removed right hydrosalpinx during laparoscopy.
The video shows the surgical site where the tube has been removed, with visible surrounding tissue and anatomical structures.
The surgical field is focused on the left side, showing anatomical structures relevant to the procedure.
The uterus is visible in the surgical field, with the left and right sides clearly identifiable.

The surgeon places a drain at the site where the hydrosalpinx and laparotomy were performed, ensuring any potential fluid collection can be safely drained, although there is no visible oozing at the moment.
